longDescription: The boundary river between Earth and the Underworld. This page is under development. More content coming soon.
name: 🌊 River Styx
shortDescription: The boundary river between Earth and the Underworld. This page is under development. More content coming soon.
description:
Explore temples, mountains, groves, and holy sites that have served as gateways between the human and divine realms
                across cultures and millennia. From ancient stone circles to living pilgrimage destinations, discover the places
                where heaven touches earth.
significance: The boundary river between Earth and the Underworld. This page is under development.

geography: A river in the Greek underworld; described as dark, murky, and fearsome; must be crossed to reach Hades proper; Charon's ferry is the only means of passage; the water itself has binding magical properties
relatedEvents: Hercules dragging Cerberus across and negotiating with Charon
Odysseus crossing to consult dead spirits
Achilles' mother bathing him in Styx (Iliad)

keyMyths.title: Achilles' Invulnerability
keyMyths.description: Thetis, Achilles' mother, dipped him into the River Styx as an infant to grant him invulnerability. However, she held him by his heel, which remained untouched by the Styx's waters, making it his only vulnerable point.

keyMyths.title: Crossing the Styx
keyMyths.description: The River Styx is the boundary between the world of the living and the Underworld. Charon, the ferryman, transports souls across the Styx to Hades' realm, but only if they have been properly buried and paid the fare (an obol).

keyMyths.title: Oaths Sworn by the Styx
keyMyths.description: The River Styx was considered a sacred and powerful entity. Gods who swore oaths upon the Styx were bound to uphold them, and breaking such an oath resulted in severe punishment, including being deprived of nectar and ambrosia for nine years.

keyMyths.title: Heracles' Twelfth Labor
keyMyths.description: As part of his twelve labors, Heracles had to descend into the Underworld and retrieve Cerberus, the three-headed dog guarding its gates. Heracles likely crossed the River Styx with the aid of Charon, although the specific details of his crossing aren't always emphasized.

keyMyths.title: The Nymph Styx
keyMyths.description: Styx was a nymph, daughter of Oceanus and Tethys, who sided with Zeus during the Titanomachy. As a reward for her loyalty, Zeus made her river the oath by which the gods swore and gave her a place of honor in the Underworld.

sources.name: Hesiod, Theogony
sources.description: A foundational text of Greek mythology, detailing the origins of the gods and their relationships.
sources.title: Hesiod, Theogony

sources.name: Homer, Odyssey
sources.description: An epic poem recounting Odysseus's journey home, including his visit to the Underworld (though the Styx is not as prominent as in other accounts).
sources.title: Homer, Odyssey

sources.name: Virgil, Aeneid Book VI
sources.description: A Latin epic poem depicting Aeneas's journey to the Underworld, providing a vivid description of the River Styx and its role.
sources.title: Virgil, Aeneid Book VI

sources.name: Apollodorus, Bibliotheca
sources.description: A comprehensive handbook of Greek mythology, offering summaries of various myths and legends.
sources.title: Apollodorus, Bibliotheca

sources.name: Statius, Achilleid
sources.description: An incomplete Latin epic poem focusing on the life of Achilles, including the story of his dipping in the River Styx.
sources.title: Statius, Achilleid
